abstract:
While the introductory material in Martin’s volume maintains a high standard of scholarship, it is occasionally undercut by two specific lapses. First, the claim that a Bachelor of Arts degree permitted Christopher Marlowe to call himself "Sir Christopher" is fundamentally misleading. Martin appears to adopt this point from Constance Kuriyama’s 2002 biography, yet this conflates a specialized academic title—the English equivalent of the Latin *Dominus*—with a social rank. As Shakespeare’s portrayal of the faux-cleric "Sir Topas" in *Twelfth Night* illustrates, such a title did not elevate a scholar to the same social standing as knights like Sir Toby Belch or Sir Andrew Aguecheek. 

Second, the introduction unfairly dismisses as "conspiracy theories" the various scholarly arguments suggesting that Marlowe’s death was a political assassination rather than a simple "quarrel over lunch." While one might disagree with the interpretations of historians like David Riggs, applying such a dismissive label is an inadequate substitute for engaging with their rigorous analyses.
